what_it_does: This file is a durable runbook and status document for the full-access remote MCP connector in the-one-application. It records the architecture, what has been built and verified, how to run the production remote.py instance, current Tailscale Funnel status, the connector URL, and remaining work.
observations: Tim's directive dated 2026-06-21 grants full authority to a remote connector so cloud apps can reach the company's 66 local tools as Tim.
Architecture is described as Identity (Supabase) -> Reach (Tailscale Funnel) -> Tools (local remote.py).
Supabase acts as the OAuth server and supports Dynamic Client Registration; remote.py only validates JWTs and serves discovery.
remote.py exposes only /mcp plus discovery, deliberately not the bridge's 50 routes, to keep the public attack surface small.
Operator-full access is keyed to OPERATOR_USER_ID ebe5f9c7-4d66-4717-835f-afc96088facb via JWT sub claim; everyone else gets 23 safe reads.
DEV_TOKEN bypass is hard-disabled when REMOTE_PUBLIC=1 to prevent static-secret bypass on the public endpoint.
An adversarial verification table is included with default-to-wrong test cases, all reported as passing on 127.0.0.1.
The production instance is currently running under nohup and is not yet a managed service.
Tailscale Funnel is configured for :10000 -> local :8772, but external TLS handshake is hanging at the funnel edge.
The tailscale cert export is kept out-of-repo at ~/.config/company/certs/ with key chmod 600; the doc warns against running tailscale cert from the repo root.
Remaining items are Funnel external routing, making remote.py a managed service, and Tim's live app-connect proof.
standouts: Single-user full authority over 66 local tools gated only by a Supabase JWT sub claim.
Explicit hard-disable of DEV_TOKEN bypass on the public instance as an advisor security catch.
Default-to-wrong adversarial verification table included directly in the runbook.
External routing is not yet live; TLS handshake hangs at Tailscale's funnel edge.
Private key export deliberately kept out-of-tree with a warning about gitignored-but-wrong placement.
conventions: Markdown status/runbook format with directive, architecture diagram, verification table, run command, and TODO list.
Security-first framing: minimal surface, identity override inside validation, default-deny verification.
Environment-variable-driven runtime configuration shown in a copy-paste shell block.
Out-of-repo secret storage recommended rather than relying solely on .gitignore.
Uses a committed hash (4859a40) to anchor the built-and-verified state.
concerns: The production connector is running as a nohup process, so it will not survive reboot or be supervised.
External routing is currently broken/non-propagated, with a suggested fallback to move from :10000 to :443.
A single user (Tim) holds full authority over dangerous MCP verbs; compromise of that Supabase account or JWT signing key grants broad local tool access.
Supabase project URL, audience, and operator UUID are written in the document, increasing exposure surface if the file is shared.
The real-token-mint proof was blocked by a safety classifier, so live full-access verification depends on Tim manually connecting an app.
The warning about not running tailscale cert from /home/tim/company implies the secret key has already been at risk of landing in the working tree.
notes: A security-oriented runbook for a one-user full-access remote MCP connector that is built and locally verified but not yet externally reachable or managed as a service.
questions: What are the exact names and capabilities of the 66 tools and the 23 safe-read tools?
Where is the source for remote.py and the bridge referenced in the file?
Who is the advisor and what process produced the listed security catches?
What does 'company-managed service' mean in this environment (systemd, container, orchestrator)?
What is the 'safety classifier' that blocked the real-token-mint proof?
Why was :10000 chosen for the Funnel instead of :443 from the start?
How is mandatory audit implemented and where are audit logs stored?
What does 'survives compaction' refer to in this repository?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is a durable runbook for the connector and contains exactly status, architecture, build/verification, run instructions, and remaining work.
intention_signals: The file is trying to establish and preserve a safe, auditable, single-operator remote MCP connector, proving its adversarial behavior before it is exposed to the public internet and before it is turned into a durable managed service.
novelty: It combines Supabase OAuth/Dynamic Client Registration, Tailscale Funnel, and a minimal local MCP front into one remote-access design, and it documents adversarial default-to-wrong verification as part of the runbook rather than as separate test code.
contribution: It contributes a durable, security-rationale-bearing runbook that records the connector's architecture, environment, verification results, known open blockers, and secret-handling rules, so a fused system can maintain and review the connector without relying on live memory.
summary_for_embedding: CONNECTOR-STATUS.md is a runbook documenting a full-access remote MCP connector that uses Supabase JWT identity and Tailscale Funnel to expose 66 local tools to a single operator while limiting others to 23 safe reads, including build verification, run instructions, and unresolved external-routing and service-management tasks.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to preserve the status, security rationale, and operational steps for a one-operator remote MCP connector so it can survive repository compaction and be handed off or maintained as a managed service.
